medications.indication_rationale: Further doses of the recently commenced metoprolol should be withheld pending urgent medical assessment, given its likely role in aggravating/unmasking the symptomatic bradycardia and syncope. This is advice to withhold an existing medication pending emergency review, not a new prescription. No exact dose is recorded because the source states only 'recently commenced low-dose' without a specific dose.
contraindications_in_this_case: Current symptomatic bradycardia (HR 42) with syncope — further beta-blocker (nodal-blocking) dosing is contraindicated pending assessment and rhythm characterisation

medications.drug_name: atropine or other emergency chronotropic/pacing intervention
medications.indication_rationale: Emergency treatments such as IV atropine, transcutaneous/temporary pacing, or (definitively) permanent pacemaker insertion may be required, but these are paramedic/emergency department/electrophysiology interventions, not something the telehealth AI Doctor prescribes or administers directly.
contraindications_in_this_case: Not administrable via telehealth — requires paramedic/ED clinical judgement, monitoring, and equipment

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do not attempt to stand, transfer or mobilise unassisted under any circumstances; remain lying flat in bed with nursing supervision and use the call bell for all needs until emergency assessment occurs.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Profound bradycardia with syncope creates a very high fall risk; keeping the patient supine also reduces cerebral hypoperfusion during bradycardic/pause episodes, helping maintain consciousness.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Withhold the next scheduled dose(s) of the recently commenced metoprolol until she has been assessed by the emergency medical team, unless otherwise directed by that team.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Continuing a nodal-blocking beta-blocker in the presence of symptomatic bradycardia and syncope risks further worsening the heart rate and precipitating a prolonged pause or arrest.

patient_education_points.point: The natural pacemaker of your heart isn't working reliably — it's swinging between beating too fast and far too slow, which is why you're feeling so weak and dizzy and why you blacked out today. This needs to be checked urgently in hospital with a heart tracing and continuous monitoring.

The new blood pressure tablet you were recently started on can slow the heart further, so please do not take your next dose until the hospital team has assessed you — they will decide what happens with it.

Please stay lying flat in bed and do not try to get up or walk to the bathroom on your own — there's a real risk of another faint and a serious fall. Let the nursing staff help you with everything, including using a bedpan if needed.

An ambulance needs to take you to hospital now for continuous heart monitoring — this can't safely wait until morning given how slow your heart is and the faint you had today. You may well need a permanent pacemaker, which the hospital team will discuss with you.

If you faint again, stop breathing, become unresponsive, or develop chest pain while waiting for the ambulance, staff should call 000 immediately for an emergency upgrade and start basic life support if you have no pulse.

follow_up_plan.interval: Immediate — emergency ambulance transfer to hospital today, no delay
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: 12-lead ECG and continuous telemetry rhythm characterisation (sinus pauses/arrest vs AV block), troponin, electrolytes/TFTs/renal function, review of the metoprolol, and inpatient electrophysiology assessment for likely permanent pacemaker insertion
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Not applicable — this case already meets the threshold for immediate emergency transfer; any further deterioration (recurrent syncope, unresponsiveness, absent pulse, chest pain) should trigger an emergency 000 upgrade and basic life support while awaiting or during transport

must_include_items: Immediate emergency ambulance transfer to hospital — not overnight observation at the facility or a next-day GP review
Explicit instruction to withhold further doses of the recently commenced metoprolol pending medical assessment
Explicit instruction to remain lying flat / not mobilise unassisted due to syncope and fall risk
Recognition that the unexplained syncope with an alternating tachy-brady pulse in the 40s is a dangerous arrhythmic emergency, not a benign faint or a simple medication side effect
Safety-netting for further deterioration (recurrent syncope, unresponsiveness, absent pulse, chest pain) triggering a 000 emergency upgrade and basic life support while awaiting transport
acceptable_alternatives: Recommending a non-emergency patient transport ambulance versus a 000 emergency ambulance may both be acceptable depending on local protocol, provided the transfer is immediate and not deferred to the next morning
Framing the cause as a heart-rhythm problem likely worsened by the new blood pressure tablet, without committing to a confirmed rhythm diagnosis, is an appropriate level of certainty for telehealth
errors_of_omission: No recommendation for emergency/urgent transfer to hospital
No instruction to withhold the recently commenced beta-blocker
No fall-risk/mobility restriction advice given the syncope
Syncope with the tachy-brady pattern not recognised as a dangerous arrhythmic emergency
No safety-netting for further deterioration provided
errors_of_commission: Agreeing to a 'watch and wait overnight, call the GP in the morning' plan
Attributing the collapse to a benign vasovagal faint and reassuring the patient home
Advising continuation of the metoprolol dose as scheduled
Recommending the patient attempt to mobilise or transfer without assistance
Fabricating or suggesting a specific new medication or dose independently rather than deferring to the receiving emergency team
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: CRITICAL CASE: Any response that does not include both (1) immediate emergency transfer to hospital AND (2) instruction to withhold further metoprolol doses pending assessment is an automatic FAIL on the management domain. This is a T5 case — unexplained syncope with a tachy-brady pulse and a resting heart rate of 42 bpm in an older patient recently started on a beta-blocker represents impending haemodynamic collapse / prolonged-pause risk.
